React is a JavaScript library used for building fast and interactive user interfaces through reusable components. It makes it easier to manage UI updates efficiently using state, props, and a component-based architecture.

The Virtual DOM is a lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM. React compares changes in the Virtual DOM first and then updates only the necessary parts of the real DOM, which improves rendering efficiency.

Props are used to pass data from a parent component to a child component, while state is used to manage data inside a component that can change over time. Props are read-only, but state is mutable through controlled updates.

Hooks are functions in React that let functional components use state, lifecycle behavior, and other React features without class components. Common examples include useState, useEffect, useRef, and useContext.

useEffect is a React hook used to handle side effects such as API calls, event listeners, timers, or DOM updates. It runs after render and can be controlled through its dependency array to avoid unnecessary executions.
useState is a React hook used to create and manage local state inside a functional component. It returns the current state value and a setter function that updates the state and triggers a re-render.

React Router is used to handle navigation between different views in a React application without reloading the page. It enables client-side routing so users can move through sections smoothly in a single-page application.

Node.js is a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome?s V8 engine that allows JavaScript to run on the server side. It is commonly used for building APIs, backend services, real-time systems, and scalable web applications.

Express.js is a lightweight web framework for Node.js that helps build APIs and backend applications quickly. It provides routing, middleware support, request-response handling, and a simple structure for server-side logic.

Middleware in Express is a function that runs between the incoming request and the final response. It is used for tasks like logging, authentication, parsing request bodies, error handling, and request validation.

A REST API is an architectural approach for building web services that communicate through HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE. It is commonly used to exchange data between frontend applications and backend systems.

GET is used to retrieve data from a server, while POST is used to send data to the server for creation or processing. GET requests usually do not modify data, while POST requests often create or submit new data.

CORS stands for Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. It is a browser security mechanism that controls whether a frontend running on one origin is allowed to access resources from a backend running on a different origin.
JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. It is a lightweight data-interchange format used to store and exchange structured data between systems in a readable key-value form.

SQL stands for Structured Query Language and is used to interact with relational databases. It helps in creating tables, inserting data, updating records, deleting records, and retrieving structured information efficiently.

SQL databases are relational and use structured tables with predefined schemas, while NoSQL databases are more flexible and can store unstructured or semi-structured data. SQL is strong for relationships and consistency, while NoSQL is often used for scalability and flexible data models.
A primary key is a column or combination of columns that uniquely identifies each row in a database table. It ensures that every record is unique and can be referenced reliably.
A foreign key is a column in one table that references the primary key of another table. It is used to create relationships between tables and maintain referential integrity in a relational database.

Normalization is the process of organizing data in a relational database to reduce redundancy and improve consistency. It involves breaking data into related tables and defining relationships properly through keys.
DBMS stands for Database Management System. It is software used to store, manage, organize, and retrieve data efficiently while supporting operations like querying, updating, security, and backup.
OOPS stands for Object-Oriented Programming System. It is a programming approach based on objects and classes, and it helps organize code in a reusable, structured, and maintainable way.
The four pillars of OOP are encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism. Together they help structure software in a way that is modular, reusable, and easier to maintain.

Encapsulation means bundling data and the methods that operate on that data inside a single unit, usually a class, while controlling access to internal details. It helps protect data and improves code maintainability.
Inheritance is an OOP concept where one class acquires the properties and behavior of another class. It helps in code reuse and allows developers to build hierarchical relationships between classes.
Polymorphism means the ability of an object or method to take multiple forms. In programming, it commonly allows the same interface or method name to behave differently depending on the context.
Abstraction means hiding implementation details and showing only the essential functionality. It helps reduce complexity and allows developers to interact with systems at a higher conceptual level.

DSA stands for Data Structures and Algorithms. It focuses on organizing data efficiently and solving problems through optimized logical steps, which helps build better and more scalable software solutions.

An array is a linear data structure used to store multiple values of the same type in contiguous memory locations. It allows fast access using an index and is commonly used for ordered collections of data.

A linked list is a linear data structure made up of nodes, where each node stores data and a reference to the next node. It is dynamic in size and useful when frequent insertions or deletions are needed.

A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO principle, which means Last In, First Out. Common operations include push, pop, and peek, and it is often used in recursion, backtracking, and expression evaluation.
A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO principle, meaning First In, First Out. It is commonly used in scheduling, buffering, and task-processing scenarios.
Time complexity measures how the execution time of an algorithm grows as the input size increases. It helps compare efficiency and is usually expressed using Big O notation.
Space complexity measures how much memory an algorithm needs as the input size grows. It includes both auxiliary space and, in some contexts, the space taken by the input itself.

Big O notation is used to describe the upper-bound growth rate of an algorithm in terms of time or space as input size increases. It helps evaluate efficiency and compare solutions in a standardized way.

Authentication verifies who a user is, while authorization determines what that authenticated user is allowed to access. In simple terms, authentication checks identity and authorization checks permissions.
JWT stands for JSON Web Token. It is a compact token-based mechanism used to securely transmit user information, commonly for authentication and authorization in web applications.

API integration means connecting one application or service with another through APIs so they can exchange data or trigger actions. It is commonly used to combine frontend interfaces with backend logic or external services.

SQLite is a lightweight relational database engine that stores the whole database in a single file. It is useful for local applications, prototypes, embedded systems, and data processing workflows where a full database server is not required.
